elements.sent-2411-001: Quantum networks are promising venues for quantum information processing.
elements.sent-2411-002: This motivates the study of the entanglement properties of the particular multipartite quantum states that underpin these structures.
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, it has been recently shown that when the links are noisy two drastically different behaviors can occur regarding the global entanglement properties of the network.
elements.sent-2411-004: While in certain configurations the network displays genuine multipartite entanglement (GME) for any system size provided the noise level is below a certain threshold, in others GME is washed out if the system size is big enough for any fixed non-zero level of noise.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, this difference has only been established considering the two extreme cases of maximally and minimally connected networks (i.e. complete graphs versus trees, respectively).
elements.sent-2411-006: In this article we investigate this question much more in depth and relate this behavior to the growth of several graph theoretic parameters that measure the connectivity of the graph sequence that codifies the structure of the network as the number of parties increases.
elements.sent-2411-007: The strongest conditions are obtained when considering the degree growth.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our main results are that a sufficiently fast degree growth (i.e.\ $\Omega(N)$, where $N$ is the size of the network) is sufficient for asymptotic robustness of GME, while if it is sufficiently slow (i.e.\ $o(\log N)$) then the network becomes asymptotically biseparable.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also present several explicit constructions related to the optimality of these results.
elements.sent-2411-010: Entanglement plays a key role both in the foundations of quantum mechanics and in the applications of quantum information theory.
elements.sent-2411-011: Thus, entanglement theory has been developed over the last decades in order to characterize and quantify this resource and to understand the possibilities and limitations for its manipulation (see e.g.\ the review article \cite{horodecki2009quantum ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, entanglement is very fragile and distributing an entangled state shared by a large number of parties is a formidable challenge in practice.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the last years, quantum networks have arisen as a very promising platform for large-scale quantum communication \cite{wehner2018quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The main idea underlying these structures is that each party (which is identified with a node in the network) controls several qudits, each of which can be entangled with a qudit held by a neighboring node.
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, each link in the network represents the ability to create bipartite entanglement between the corresponding pair of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-016: Then, the use of quantum repeaters and teleportation makes it possible to establish long-distance quantum communication and to distribute to the parties any multipartite state that might be necessary for some specific quantum information task.
elements.sent-2411-017: This approach needs to consume many instances of the pre-shared entanglement and, thus, the entangled links need to be permanently replenished, which leads to the study of entanglement routing techniques (see e.g.\ \cite{elkouss and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, if entangled links are not refreshed and are modelled by a particular bipartite state, each network pattern defines a multipartite quantum state, which can be understood as a fixed resource state.
elements.sent-2411-019: This state is modelled by an undirected simple graph where the parties correspond to vertices (nodes) and edges (links) represent that a pair of parties share some form of bipartite entanglement to be specified.
elements.sent-2411-020: This view is more natural from the perspective of entanglement theory and leads to the investigation of the entanglement properties of multipartite network states.
elements.sent-2411-021: This approach has been taken for instance in the study of the phenomenon of entanglement percolation \cite{percolation .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Here, one studies which network states enable to establish with non-zero probability a perfect 2-qubit maximally entangled state between a pair of arbitrarily distant parties within the network configuration by local operations and classical communication (LOCC) (see the review \cite{percolationreview and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-023: Another example is given by the recent work \cite{pairable , which shows that a $N$-partite network state with $O(\log N)$ qubits per party in a precise configuration gives a construction of a maximally pairable state, i.e.\ for any choice of grouping of the parties into disjoint pairs, a state from which by LOCC one can obtain 2-qubit maximally entangled states shared by each pair.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In addition to this, references \cite{yamasaki and \cite{kraftspee have studied general LOCC transformations starting from network states where the links correspond to maximally entangled states.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: On a different note, \cite{networkGMNL shows that all noiseless network states arising from a connected graph are genuine multipartite nonlocal and \cite{ASGME uses noisy quantum network states to prove that genuine multipartite nonlocality can be superactivated for any number of parties.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: A natural and interesting question in the above direction is to study when a network state displays entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-027: Obviously, the parties corresponding to nodes connected by a link can be entangled, but can this be extended to global entanglement that is spread through the whole network? This notion is precisely captured by the concept of genuine multipartite entanglement (GME).
elements.sent-2411-028: In fact, GME is known to be necessary for maximum sensitivity in quantum metrology \cite{gmesensing1,gmesensing2 and to obtain multipartite secret key \cite{gmekey .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Indeed, most relevant states that appear in applications, such as for example graph states, are GME.
elements.sent-2411-030: Interestingly, since the set of biseparable (BS) states (i.e.\ non-GME states) is closed by LOCC, any network state that is not GME is severely limited for many applications as it is impossible to obtain any GME state from it by further LOCC processing.
elements.sent-2411-031: It is easy to see that any network state corresponding to a connected graph and where the edges correspond to pure bipartite entangled states has to be GME.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, an interesting phenomenon arises when the nodes share mixed states.
elements.sent-2411-033: This can already be seen by considering the simple and standard noise model in which every edge describes an isotropic state shared by the adjacent vertices with visibility $p$ (where $p=1$ corresponds to the ideal case of a pure maximally entangled state; see \cite{isotropic ).}
elements.sent-2411-034: In \cite{ASGME it has been shown that the properties of the graph codifying the structure of the network play a non-trivial role in this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-035: To wit, it turns out that for any constant $p<1$ there exist graph sequences such that the corresponding network state is not GME for a large enough size.
elements.sent-2411-036: That is, the slightest form of noise renders the network state biseparable if the number of parties is sufficiently large.
elements.sent-2411-037: Remarkably, on the other hand, there also exist graph sequences for which the corresponding network state has an asymptotically robust GME, i.e.\ GME can be displayed for any system size for some fixed $p<1$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Thus, if the devices that produce entangled pairs between nodes are sufficiently developed as to guarantee a visibility above a certain threshold, one can find network configurations that are GME for any number of parties.
elements.sent-2411-039: On the other hand, other network configurations are doomed to failure and fundamentally limited in applications since in any realistic scenario the entangling devices will always operate outside the ideal case of a perfect visibility ($p=1$).
elements.sent-2411-040: Intuitively, the more connected a network is the more likely it is to have robust multipartite entanglement properties.
elements.sent-2411-041: In fact, \cite{ASGME proved the existence of these two different behaviors by only considering the extreme cases of tree graphs (minimally connected) and completely connected graphs (maximally connected).}
elements.sent-2411-042: This, however, leaves wide open a finer analysis of the relation between asymptotic robustness of GME in noisy network states of increasing size and different notions of graph connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this article, we combine quantum information and graph theory techniques to study systematically and in more depth this problem, unveiling a subtle and non-trivial relation.
elements.sent-2411-044: The most standard measures of the connectivity of a graph are the vertex-connectivity and the edge-connectivity \cite{diestel,bondy .}
elements.sent-2411-045: While we find that when these quantities grow sufficiently fast this ensures that the network has asymptotically robust GME, on the other hand, it turns out that minimally connected graph sequences as measured by these two parameters can also have this property.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our analysis reveals that the degree growth of the graph sequence, which is also intimately connected to the connectivity of the graphs, gives the strongest results providing sufficient conditions for both asymptotic GME and asymptotic biseparability.
elements.sent-2411-047: Yet, we prove that this parameter alone cannot fully characterize these properties.
elements.sent-2411-048: Although precise definitions will be given below (see Section~\ref{sec2 ), we will briefly introduce here the minimum necessary notions for understanding the main results of the article.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We consider simple, connected and undirected graphs $G=(V,E)$ with order $|G|=|V|$.
elements.sent-2411-050: Given a vertex $v\in V$ we denote its degree (the number of vertices adjacent to $v$) by $\delta(v)$ and define the maximal and minimal degrees of $G$ respectively by \begin{equation}\delta_{\mathrm{min}}(G)=\min_{v\in V}\delta(v) \quadtext{and} \delta_{\mathrm{max}}(G)=\max_{v\in V}\delta(v).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-051: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$ as above, a dimension $d\in\mathbb{N}$ ($d\geq2$) and a visibility parameter $p\in[0,1]$, the isotropic network state is the $|G|$-partite density matrix given by \begin{equation}\sigma_d(G,p)= \bigotimes_{e\in E}\rho_e(p,d),\end{equation}where the subscript $e=(u,v)$ denotes that the isotropic state is shared by the parties $u$ and $v$.
elements.sent-2411-052: The state $\sigma_d$ corresponds to a $|G|$-partite state described by the graph $G$ where the vertices are identified with the parties and the edges are identified with bipartite isotropic states of parameter $p$, shared by the corresponding adjacent parties.
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus, for any given values of $p$ and $d$ we can describe any sequence of isotropic network states with growing size by a sequence $\cG=(G_n)_{n\in\N}$ of (finite, simple and connected) graphs $G_n=(V_n,E_n)$ such that the order $|G_n|$ tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-054: The multipartite entanglement robustness properties that we want to study are precisely captured by the notions of asymptotically genuine multipartite entanglement (AGME) and asymptotic biseparability (ABS), as introduced in Definition \ref{def:asymptotic below and that rigorously establish the intuitive ideas discussed in the previous paragraphs.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The main results in this article (where we use standard asymptotic notation) give sufficient conditions guaranteeing either AGME or ABS behaviors, which go well beyond the mere existence of the phenomenon that was established in \cite{ASGME .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Theorem~\ref{th:degAGME ] Any graph sequence $\cG=(G_n)_{n\in\N}$ such that $\delta_{\mathrm{min (G_n)\in\Omega(|G_n|)$ is AGME.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Theorem~\ref{th:degABS ] Any graph sequence $\cG=(G_n)_{n\in\N}$ such that $\delta_{\mathrm{max (G_n)\in o\left(\log|G_n|\right)$ is ABS.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Theorem~\ref{th:highdegABS ] For any function $f\colon\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that $f(n)\in o(n)$, there exists an ABS graph sequence $\mathcal{G =(G_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N }$ such that $\delta_{\mathrm{min (G_n)\in\Omega(f(|G_n|))$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Theorem~\ref{th:lowdegAGME ] For any $\alpha\in(0,1]$, there exists an AGME sequence of regular graphs $\cG=(G_n)_{n\in\N}$ such that $\delta_{\mathrm{max (G_n)\in O(|G_n|^\alpha)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Thus, Theorem~\ref{thm:2 proves that AGME requires that this quantity grows as the network gets bigger (and, in fact, it has to grow sufficiently fast).}
elements.sent-2411-061: On the other hand, Theorem~\ref{thm:1 shows that if it grows fast enough, then AGME is guaranteed and Theorem~\ref{thm:3 shows that this latter condition cannot be improved.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Theorem~\ref{thm:4 gives instances of asymptotically robust GME networks with the lowest overhead in terms of local dimension that we have been able to find and together with Theorem~\ref{thm:2 leaves only a relatively small gap for improvements.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Moreover, Theorems~\ref{thm:3 and \ref{thm:4 prove that the AGME and ABS behaviors cannot be fully characterized only in terms of the degree.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In addition to this, we relate these notions to the connectivity of the graph sequence as measured by the vertex and edge connectivities, obtaining this same conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-065: In fact, when these quantities grow sufficiently fast they also guarantee AGME but give a weaker condition to that of Theorem~\ref{thm:1 (however, this serves as an important technical ingredient in the proofs of our main results).}
elements.sent-2411-066: On the contrary, they can never give a sufficient condition for ABS.
elements.sent-2411-067: This is because we can construct minimally connected graph sequences which are nevertheless AGME (see Proposition~\ref{th:lowlambda and Fig.~\ref{fig:mancuerna ).}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Thus, our analysis indicates that the AGME and ABS properties represent an alternative notion of connectivity in graphs that can be related to these and other relevant graph parameters, but that seems to be ultimately independent of them.
elements.sent-2411-069: We therefore think that this problem provides a fruitful mathematical interplay between entanglement theory and graph theory that deserves further study.

elements.section-2411-002: Basic definitions and properties of quantum states and networks
elements.sent-2411-076: In this section we review the notions of quantum theory and graph theory that will be used throughout the article.
elements.sent-2411-077: In our analysis we use as well standard asymptotic notation.
elements.sent-2411-078: Given two functions $f\colon\mathbb N\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ and $g\colon\mathbb N\rightarrow[0,\infty)$ (such that $g(n)>0$ for large enough $n$) we write $f(n)\in O(g(n))$ if there exists a real constant $c>0$ and an integer $n_0\geq 1$ such that $|f(n)|\leq cg(n)$ for every $n\geq n_0$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Equivalently, $f(n)\in O(g(n))$ (resp.\ $f(n)\in \Omega(g(n))$) if and only if (iff) $\limsup_{n\to\infty (|f(n)|/g(n))<\infty$ (resp.\ $\liminf_{n\to\infty (f(n)/g(n))>0$).}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We write $f(n)\in o(g(n))$ if for every real constant $c>0$ there exists an integer $n_0\geq 1$ such that $|f(n)|\leq cg(n)$ for every $n\geq n_0$ and $f(n)\in \omega(g(n))$ if $g(n)\in o(f(n))$.
elements.sent-2411-083: In this subsection we introduce the basic definitions and results on quantum states that will be needed in the article.
elements.sent-2411-085: We will consider here finite-dimensional quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-086: The (complex) Hilbert spaces $H$ will have dimension $d\in\{2,3,\dots \}$ and we denote by $\{|i\rangle\}_{i=1 ^d$ an orthonormal basis of $H$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Given $H$, quantum states are characterized by density matrices, that is, by linear operators $\rho\colon H\to H$ such that $\rho$ is positive semidefinite (from now on, $\rho\geq 0$) and $\tr\rho=1$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The set of all density matrices will be denoted by $D(H)\subset B(H)$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Quantum channels $\Lambda\colon D(H)\to D(H')$ are given by completely positive and trace preserving linear maps.
elements.sent-2411-090: Given $\rho,\sigma\in D(H)$, their fidelity is defined by \begin{equation}\label{fidelity} F(\rho,\sigma)=\tr^2\sqrt{\sqrt{\rho}\sigma\sqrt{\rho}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-091: For bipartite quantum systems, the associated Hilbert space is $H=H_1\otimes H_2$.
elements.sent-2411-092: A state with density matrix $\rho\in D(H)$ is separable if it can be written as \begin{equation}\label{def:separable} \rho=\sum_{j}p_j\sigma_j\otimes\tau_j,\end{equation} where for all $j$, $p_j\geq0$ with $\sum_jp_j=1$, and $\sigma_j\in D(H_1)$, $\tau_j\in D(H_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-093: The set of separable density matrices in $D(H)$ is denoted by $S(H)$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Density matrices that are not separable are known as entangled.
elements.sent-2411-095: It is well known that the set of separable states is closed under LOCC quantum channels (see \cite{locc for a precise definition), i.e.\ $S(H)$ is LOCC-stable.}
elements.sent-2411-096: We will often consider the following particular state $\rho(p,d)\in D(H_1\otimes H_2)$ with $\dim H_1=\dim H_2=d\in\mathbb{N}$ ($d\geq2$), which is known as the isotropic state \cite{isotropic and is given by \begin{equation}\label{isotropicd} \rho(p,d)=p\phi^+_d+(1-p)\none_{d^2},\end{equation} where $\one_{d^2}$ is the identity operator on $H=H_1\otimes H_2$ and $\none_{d^2 =\one_{d^2 /d^2$ denotes the normalized identity operator.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Moreover, \begin{equation}\label{maxent} |\phi^+_d\rangle=\frac{1}{\sqrt{d}} \sum_{i=1}^d|ii\rangle\in H_1\otimes H_2 \quadtext{and} \phi^+_d=|\phi^+_d\rangle\langle\phi^+_d|\in D(H_1\otimes H_2)\end{equation} is the rank~$1$ projection corresponding to the $d$-dimensional maximally entangled state.
elements.sent-2411-098: Here and in the following, we will use the short-hand $|ij\rangle=|i\rangle\otimes|j\rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-099: The parameter $p\in[0,1]$ in Eq.~(\ref{isotropicd ) is often referred to as visibility and it might be understood as a measure of the quality with which a maximally entangled state is prepared when our set-up introduces white noise.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Notice that the completely depolarizing quantum channel in $D(H_1\otimes H_2)$ given by $\Lambda(X)=\tr(X)\none_{d^2}$, is an LOCC channel and that the set of LOCC channels is convex \cite{locc .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Thus, whenever $p'\leq p$ there always exists an LOCC channel $\Lambda$ such that $\Lambda(\rho(p,d))=\rho(p',d)$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Since $S(H)$ is a LOCC-stable subset of the state space, $\rho(1,d)$ is entangled and $\rho(0,d)$ is separable for every dimension $d$, we conclude that there is a critical visibility $\overline{p}$ separating, for each isotropic state, entanglement from separability.
elements.sent-2411-103: Actually, it is shown in \cite{isotropic that $\rho(p,d)$ is entangled iff \begin{equation}\label{isotropicthreshold} p>\frac{1}{d+1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Any state $\rho\in D(H_1\otimes H_2)$ has a convex decomposition as \begin{equation}\label{bsa1} \rho=\lambda\sigma+(1-\lambda)\tau\;,\end{equation} where $\sigma\in S(H_1\otimes H_2)$ is separable, $\tau\in D(H_1\otimes H_2)$ and $\lambda\in[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-105: The notion of best separable approximation (see \cite{BSA ) is defined as \begin{equation}\label{bsa2} \mathrm{BSA}(\rho)=\max\left\{\lambda\in[0,1]\mid \rho=\lambda\sigma+(1-\lambda)\tau, \sigma\in S(H), \tau\in D(H)\right\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The quantity $E_{\mathrm{BSA (\rho)=1-\mathrm{BSA (\rho)$ is an entanglement measure and it cannot increase by LOCC transformations (see \cite{pleniovirmani ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: In particular, results in \cite{BSAisotropic show that for isotropic states given in Eq.~(\ref{isotropicd ) \begin{equation}\label{bsaisotropic} \mathrm{BSA}(\rho(p,d))=\frac{d+1}{d}(1-p)\;, \quadtext{for} p\geq 1/(d+1)\;.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: For multipartite quantum systems with $N$ parties, we denote $[N]=\{1,2,\ldots,N\}$ and the corresponding Hilbert space is $H=\bigotimes_{i=1 ^N H_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-109: A density matrix $\chi\in D(H)$ on $H=\bigotimes_{i=1 ^N H_i$ is said to be separable in the nontrivial bipartition $M|\overline{M}$, (where $\emptyset\not=M\subsetneq[N]$ and $\overline{M}$ is the complement of $M$ in $[N]$), if it has a convex decomposition of the form \begin{equation}\chi=\sum_{j}p_j\sigma_j\otimes\tau_j\,, \quadtext{with} p_j\geq0 \quadtext{and}\sum_jp_j=1\;,\end{equation} and where $\sigma_j\in D\left(\bigotimes_{i\in M H_i\right)$ and $\tau_j\in D\left(\bigotimes_{i\notin M H_i\right)$ for all $j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The set of all density matrices with this property is denoted by $S_M(H)$.
elements.sent-2411-111: The set of biseparable density matrices (BS) is the convex hull of the union of these sets \begin{equation}\label{def:bs} \mathrm{BS}(H)=\mathrm{conv}\left\{\bigcup_{\emptyset\neq M\subsetneq[N]}S_M(H)\right\}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: A state that is not biseparable is called genuine multipartite entangled (GME).
elements.sent-2411-113: Note that a density matrix $\rho\in D(H)$ is biseparable iff \begin{equation}\label{def:biseparable} \rho=\sum_{\emptyset\neq M\subsetneq[N]}p_M\chi_M,\end{equation} where for any $M$ we have $p_M\geq0$ with $\sum_Mp_M=1$ and $\chi_M\in S_M(H)$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Notice that it suffices to consider in Eqs.~(\ref{def:bs ) and (\ref{def:biseparable ) only subsets $M$ that give rise to different bipartitions, but to simplify the notation we do not impose this constraint explicitly.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: It follows from the LOCC-stability of the set of separable states that the set of biseparable states is LOCC-stable as well.
elements.sent-2411-116: In the proof of the main result in Section~\ref{subsecdeg1 it will be convenient to introduce the particular notion of $1$-biseparable states, i.e., biseparable states where the convex decomposition in Eq.~(\ref{def:biseparable ) runs over all subsets $M$ with cardinality one, i.e., $|M|=1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: The set of states which are separable in the bipartition specified by $M=\{j\}$ are denoted simply by $S_j(H)$ and, hence, the set of 1-biseparable states is $\mathrm{conv}\{\bigcup_{j\in[N] S_j(H)\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Given then an $N$-party Hilbert space $H=\bigotimes_{i=1 ^N H_i$ and a non-empty subset $M\subsetneq[N]$, the partial trace with respect to $\bigotimes_{i\in M H_i$ (or simply with respect to $M$) is defined to be the linear operator \begin{equation}\tr_{(\bigotimes_{i\in M}H_i)}\colon B(H)\to B\big(\otimes_{i\notin M}H_i\big)\end{equation} such that \begin{equation}\tr_{\left(\bigotimes_{i\in M}H_i\right)}\left(\bigotimes_{i=1}^NA_i\right)=\tr\left(\bigotimes_{i\in M}A_i\right)\bigotimes_{i\notin M}A_i,\end{equation} for any $A_i\in B(H_i)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Since graph theoretical concepts play an important role in our analysis we begin recalling some basic notions and results on graphs needed later (see e.g., \cite{diestel,bondy ).}
elements.sent-2411-120: In this article, a graph is a finite and simple graph (no loops nor multiple edges are allowed) given by a pair $G = (V, E)$ consisting of two disjoint finite sets $V$, the set of vertices or nodes, and $E$ the set of edges or links.
elements.sent-2411-121: The elements of $E$ are unordered pairs $(u, v)$ of vertices $u, v \in V$ which we will also denote by $e,f\dots$.
elements.sent-2411-122: If $e=(u,v) \in E$, we say that $u$ is adjacent to $v$ (or that $u$ is a neighbour of $v$ and we denote the vertex neighborhood of $v$ by $N(v)$); moreover, we say that $e$ is adjacent to $u$ (and to $v$).
elements.sent-2411-123: The cardinality of the vertex set is the order of the graph which we denote by $|G|=|V|$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Sometimes it will be convenient to numerate the vertices and identify $V=[|G|]$.
elements.sent-2411-125: A path is a simple graph whose vertices can be arranged in a linear sequence so that two vertices are adjacent if they are consecutive in the sequence and are nonadjacent otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-126: A graph $G$ is connected if any pair of vertices can be linked by a path in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-127: A maximal connected subgraph of $G$ is called a connected component of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-128: The distance $d_G(u,v)$ between two vertices $u,v\in V$ of a connected graph is the minimal length of the paths in $G$ joining $u$ with $v$.
elements.sent-2411-129: The diameter of the graph is then defined by \begin{equation}\mathrm{diam}(G):=\max_{u,v\in V}d_G (u,v)\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: Given two subsets of vertices $V_1,V_2\subset V$, we denote the set of $V_1$-$V_2$ edges by \begin{equation}E(V_1,V_2):=\{ (v_1,v_2) \mid v_i \in V_i \;,\; i=1,2 \} \quadtext{and} E(V_1):=E(V_1,V_1);.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-131: We also denote, for simplicity, all edges adjacent to a vertex $v\in V$ as \begin{equation}E(v):=E\big(\{v\},V\big)\quadtext{and define the degree of $v$ by} \delta(v):=|E(v)|\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-132: The minimal and maximal degree of $G$ are given respectively by \begin{equation}\label{maxmindegree} \delta_{\mathrm{min}}(G)=\min_{v\in V}\delta(v) \quadtext{and} \delta_{\mathrm{max}}(G)=\max_{v\in V}\delta(v).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-133: A subgraph $G'$ of a graph $G$ is a graph formed from a subset of the vertices and of edges of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-134: A subgraph is called spanning if $V'=V$, i.e., it contains all vertices from $G$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Edge deletion is a natural procedure to obtain a subgraph from a given graph.
elements.sent-2411-136: An induced subgraph $G'$ of $G$ is a subgraph that includes all the edges with adjacent vertices in $V'$.
elements.sent-2411-137: Given the vertex subset $V'\subset V$ the subgraph induced by $V'$ is the subgraph $G'=(V',E')$ with $E'=E(V')$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Vertex deletion of $G$ specifies an induced subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-139: In this article, the graph connectivity of the graph associated to a network state will play an important role.
elements.sent-2411-140: There are two basic notions of connectivity for a graph $G$: the vertex-connectivity $\kappa(G)$ and the edge-connectivity $\lambda(G)$ (see e.g. \cite{diestel,bondy ).}
elements.sent-2411-141: Recall that an edge-cut of $G$ (and similarly for a vertex-cut) is a subset of edges that, when removed, the remaining graph has at least two connected components.

elements.sent-2411-577: However, our results prove that these notions do not play a relevant role in the AGME/ABS question.
elements.sent-2411-578: Graph expanders can have bounded degree and, in fact, there exist constructions of expander sequences of $k$-regular graphs with $k\geq 3$ (see \cite{marcus15 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-579: Therefore, Theorem~\ref{th:degABS entails that these objects do not necessarily lead to AGME.}
elements.sent-2411-580: On the other hand, the graph sequences used in Proposition~\ref{th:lowlambda have Cheeger constant going to zero as $n\to\infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-581: Therefore, algebraic connectivity alone cannot give sufficient conditions for the ABS property either.
elements.sent-2411-582: However, other graph parameters might be suitable for capturing the behavior of multipartite entanglement addressed in this work.
elements.sent-2411-583: Once the underlying dimension $d$ is fixed, if a graph sequence is AGME, this entails that there exists a value of the visibility $p<1$ such that the corresponding isotropic network state $\sigma_d(G_n,p)$ is GME for all $n$.
elements.sent-2411-584: However, the mere property of being AGME does not say anything about how far from 1 the corresponding visibility can be, which is a question of obvious practical relevance.
elements.sent-2411-585: Thus, it would be interesting to derive tools to address the problem of determining the threshold visibility of a given AGME graph sequence, i.e.\ the infimum over all $p_0$ for which the property that defines AGME as given in Definition~\ref{def:asymptotic holds.}
elements.sent-2411-586: Another feature that we have not touched upon here is the role of the dimension $d$ of the bipartite entanglement sources that give rise to the links in the network.
elements.sent-2411-587: The definitions of AGME and ABS depend on this choice; however, all the conditions that we obtained here are independent of this parameter.
elements.sent-2411-588: We leave for future study the question of whether there exists a graph sequence that is AGME or ABS (or none of the two) depending on the choice of $d$ or whether the dimension plays no role in this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-589: In summary, we believe that the AGME/ABS problem in quantum networks unveils a mathematically rich scenario in the intersection of quantum information theory and graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-590: While we have shown here that these properties can be related to basic quantities in graph theory, no such notion seems to be able to completely characterize them.
elements.sent-2411-591: Therefore, we think that AGME leads to a novel graph parameter related to a different notion of connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-592: We hope that our work spurs further research in this direction.
